definition: Gradient descent that estimates the gradient from a small random mini-batch instead of the whole dataset, trading an exact gradient for thousands of cheap, noisy steps. The startling part is that the noise is a feature, not a bug: it shakes the optimizer out of traps and biases it toward solutions that generalize.

body.text: Stochastic gradient descent rests on a concession that turns out to be an advantage: you do not need the exact gradient to train a model, only a cheap, noisy estimate of it. Plain batch gradient descent computes the gradient of the loss over the entire training set before taking one step, accurate but hopelessly slow once a dataset runs to millions of examples. SGD instead draws a small random mini-batch, estimates the gradient from just that sample, and updates immediately, making thousands of small steps per pass through the data where batch descent would make one. It is the practical form of gradient descent that trains essentially every modern neural network.

The "stochastic" in the name is the sampling noise, and the surprise is that the noise helps. Each mini-batch gradient is correct on average but wrong on any single step, and that jitter lets the optimizer escape shallow traps an exact method would settle into, knocking the parameters out of a poor local minimum or off a saddle point where the true gradient is nearly zero. The same noise is why a training loss curve is jagged rather than smooth, and there is good evidence it biases the optimizer toward flatter, wider minima, which tend to generalize better. A bug by the standards of pure optimization is a feature by the standards of learning.

Each update still follows the gradient descent rule, a small step against the estimated gradient scaled by the learning rate, and the mini-batch size is the knob trading noise for throughput. Larger batches give smoother, more reliable estimates and use hardware more efficiently but take fewer steps per pass and can drift toward sharper, less generalizable solutions; smaller batches are noisier but often generalize better. Because the noise scale and the step size interact, the learning rate usually has to be retuned whenever the batch size changes, a coupling that trips up practitioners who scale one without the other.

SGD is the foundation the accelerated and adaptive optimizers are built on. Momentum smooths the noisy trajectory by averaging recent gradients; Adam and RMSprop additionally rescale each parameter's step by a running estimate of its gradient magnitude. A learning-rate schedule is almost always layered on top, lowering the step size over training so the noise that powered early exploration does not stop the model from finally settling into a minimum. Strip those refinements away and what remains, a noisy step downhill on a handful of examples, is still the move that does the actual learning.

image.alt: Contour plot of a bowl-shaped objective with a stochastic gradient descent path that descends toward the center and then jitters in a noisy cloud around the minimum.
image.caption: Stochastic gradient descent uses a noisy gradient from a small random sample each step, so its path descends roughly toward the minimum and then jitters around it rather than settling exactly. That same noise is what lets it escape shallow traps a smooth method would stall in.
image.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.
